text: The surgeon begins by restoring normal anatomy, dividing lateral attachments to facilitate medial to lateral dissection. The recto-sigma junction is elevated in an anterior orientation, tenting the inferior mesenteric artery (IMA) pedicle at a ninety-degree angle to the aorta.

The surgeon performs lateral attachment dissection, searching for the interdigitating sigmoid fossa. The left ureter is visible deep to the fossa. The dissection transitions from lateral to medial orientation, marking the start of the case.

The surgeon uses the third arm of the robotic system to tent the recto-sigma junction anteriorly, positioning it beneath the second arm to fan out the mesentery and lift the IMA pedicle. The mesorectum, visible as a light yellowish tissue, is differentiated from the darker yellow retroperitoneum. The surgeon jiggles the peritoneum to predict the junction between these tissues, preparing for a medial lateral incision that results in a plume of pneumodissection into the presacral space.

The surgeon begins the medial to lateral dissection, identifying and preserving the hypogastric nerves by pushing them down. The third robotic arm remains stationary while the second arm lifts the mesorectum anteriorly. The surgeon uses a poke, spread, and lift maneuver with the left hand to avoid damaging the mesentery. The left ureter is located in the pre-circular space, and the dissection continues proximally towards the takeoff of the inferior mesenteric artery (IMA) from the aorta.

The surgeon exchanges the third robotic arm to lift the dissection point, moving proximally to take down embryological attachments. The inferior mesenteric artery (IMA) is peeled off the retroperitoneum and aorta, revealing the T-shaped junction where the IMA meets the aorta. The surgeon uses scissors to create a window around the IMA pedicle, with the aorta visible at the bottom of the screen and the descending mesocolon above. The inferior mesenteric vein (IMV) is visible, and the left colic artery is identified to the left of the screen.

The surgeon uses a vessel sealer to divide the IMA pedicle, sealing it multiple times before cutting. The divided pedicle is handed to the third robotic arm and lifted anteriorly towards the abdominal wall. The surgeon continues the medial dissection, pushing down retroperitoneal structures, including the left ureter and Girota's fascia, while progressing laterally. Occasional bleeding indicates deviation from the correct dissection plane.

The surgeon carefully dissects the descending mesocolon, noting its thin appearance even in heavier patients. They identify a clear tissue plane and ensure blood vessels are directed downward while the yellow adipose tissue is directed upward. The surgeon uses the third robotic arm to perform the poke, spread, and lift maneuver, pushing down retroperitoneal attachments and working laterally to achieve sufficient lateral and proximal mobilization of the mesocolon.

The surgeon uses scissors to make a full-thickness incision along the lateral attachments of the sigmoid colon, aiming to connect with the previously dissected medial plane. The floppy sigmoid descending colon is visible, and the surgeon carefully separates filmy lateral attachments from the midpoint of the sigmoid colon upwards, potentially reaching the splenic flexure.

The surgeon completes the proximal dissection and proceeds distally, entering the pre-circular space to divide the lateral attachments. The dissection is extended distally to the point where it meets the initial dissection on the right-hand side, ensuring symmetry in the low anterior resection. The left and right hands are used to pull the mesorectum anteriorly.

The third arm of the surgical robot is positioned with the tip pointing away, grasping the mesorectum at the recto sigmoid junction to provide exposure to the pelvis. The surgeon begins a tumor-specific mesorectal excision (TME) due to the presence of a recto sigmoid tumor. The left hand is used to lift the mesorectum anteriorly in a circular motion, carefully avoiding the presacral venous plexus and hypogastric nerve plexus, which are visible as filmy structures.

The surgeon uses the left hand at a right angle to poke and lift the mesorectum anteriorly towards the abdominal wall, then pulls towards the head in a circular motion. This technique is applied to mature the mesorectal dissection, starting at the posterior midline and moving to the left, while providing counter traction by pushing the left-handed instrument to the right. The same method is used on the right side, pulling the mesorectum to the left to avoid damaging the pelvic sidewall. The third arm is repositioned to assist in taking down the lateral attachments distal to the tumor.

The surgeon uses a bipolar device to perform symmetric dissection on the left and right sides, starting at the center and avoiding the pre-circular venous plexus. The posterior mesorectal dissection is continued, with the colonoscope set up to verify the distal margin of the tumor. The endoscopic image shows the lower edge of the tumor, confirming the planned division below the tattoo.

The surgeon scores the mesorectum circumferentially to establish landmarks for division, using scissors for the posterior mesorectal dissection. The rectum is visible, and the surgeon identifies and divides the superior haremortal using a vessel sealant. The mesorectal attachments are finished perpendicular to the rectum, preparing for stapler use.

The surgeon uses a green load stapler to divide the proximal rectum, ensuring complete clamping before firing. The mesorectum and rectum are divided, and the vessel sealer is used to rotate the sigmoid colon out of the pelvis. The descending sigmoid junction is tented to identify the anastomotic site, and the specimen is flipped back into the pelvis. The proximal division point of the IMA pedicle is located, keeping it with the specimen for lymph node harvesting.

The surgeon uses a vessel sealer to double burn the mesentery from the IMA pedicle to the third arm, ensuring precise division of the mesentery. The third arm is used as a reference point to avoid accidental division of the descending colon or recto sigmoid. The final attachments of the mesentery are skinned down with the vessel sealer. An intravenous injection of 2.5 cc's of ICG with a 10 cc flush is administered, illuminating the surgical field. An anvil is inserted into the abdomen through an extended skin incision.

The surgeon divides the descending sigmoid junction using a vessel sealer, achieving a straight cut. The three ovula are placed in the abdomen. The surgeon begins suturing at the seven o'clock position on the descending colon conduit using a needle driver, performing a baseball stitch in an into-out fashion. The suturing progresses in one-centimeter increments with half-centimeter bites, using the fenestrated bipolar instrument for measurement reference.

The surgeon uses the third robotic arm to hold the descending colon conduit at the twelve o'clock position while the left hand pulls down at the six o'clock position to secure the anvil in place. The suture is cinched tightly around the base of the anvil, and a second round of suturing is performed using a V-lock suture. The mesentery is cleaned off using scissors, and the robotic technique mirrors the open case approach. The mesentery is skinned down symmetrically, ensuring hemostasis and proper orientation. The specimen is placed in the right lower quadrant, and the assistant begins transdental dilatation with EA sizers.

The surgeon inserts the stapler to the splenic vein area, ensuring adequate length for the rectum mobilization. The camera is cleaned, and the assistant positions the EA sensors. The stapler is prepared at double speed, with the spike positioned to one side of the rectal cuff to minimize intersecting staple lines. The anvil spike is brought through the left side of the rectal stump, ensuring proper mesenteric orientation. This technique aims to reduce the risk of anastomotic leaks by limiting intersecting staple lines.

The surgeon uses an E-A sizer to fire, while the assistant prepares to bring the colonoscope up. The pelvis is filled with saline, and the third arm clamps off to ensure hemostasis. ICG is reinjected, showing good perfusion of the rectum and sigmoid. The endoscopic view reveals the anastomosis, which appears intact and not bleeding. An interrupted suture is placed on the left side of the anastomosis to address a thermal mark. The vessel sealer is used off-label as a needle driver to suture the area. The specimen is placed in a bag and removed through the staple port extension, concluding the procedure.
